content:
Hello all!

A client asked me to teach them a bit about how Rust for Linux works, so I've been trying to prepare a demo. A found a bunch of guides on-line for running Linux in QEMU and they're all different, and I fell over a dozen showstoppers, but I think I have something that works - see https://gist.github.com/jonathanpallant/3a348d54bcd0449aadd4357383fda0d8.

However, I've installed Linux 6.14 and https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module gives me:

root@localhost:~/rust-out-of-tree-module# make KDIR=../linux-6.14 LLVM=1
make -C ../linux-6.14 M=$PWD
make[1]: Entering directory '/root/linux-6.14'
make[2]: Entering directory '/root/rust-out-of-tree-module'
  RUSTC [M] rust_out_of_tree.o
error: proc macro panicked
  --> rust_out_of_tree.rs:7:1
   |
7  | / module! {
8  | |     type: RustOutOfTree,
9  | |     name: "rust_out_of_tree",
10 | |     authors: ["Rust for Linux Contributors"],
11 | |     description: "Rust out-of-tree sample",
12 | |     license: "GPL",
13 | | }
   | |_^
   |
   = help: message: Unknown key "authors". Valid keys are: ["type", "name", "author", "description", "license", "alias", "firmware"].

error[E0425]: cannot find value `__LOG_PREFIX` in the crate root
  --> rust_out_of_tree.rs:21:9
   |
21 |         pr_info!("Rust out-of-tree sample (init)\n");
   |         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ not found in the crate root
   |
   = note: this error originates in the macro `$crate::print_macro` which comes from the expansion of the macro `pr_info` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

error[E0425]: cannot find value `__LOG_PREFIX` in the crate root
  --> rust_out_of_tree.rs:34:9
   |
34 |         pr_info!("My numbers are {:?}\n", self.numbers);
   |         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ not found in the crate root
   |
   = note: this error originates in the macro `$crate::print_macro` which comes from the expansion of the macro `pr_info` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

error[E0425]: cannot find value `__LOG_PREFIX` in the crate root
  --> rust_out_of_tree.rs:35:9
   |
35 |         pr_info!("Rust out-of-tree sample (exit)\n");
   |         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ not found in the crate root
   |
   = note: this error originates in the macro `$crate::print_macro` which comes from the expansion of the macro `pr_info` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

error: aborting due to 4 previous errors

For more information about this error, try `rustc --explain E0425`.
make[4]: *** [/root/linux-6.14/scripts/Makefile.build:263: rust_out_of_tree.o] Error 1
make[3]: *** [/root/linux-6.14/Makefile:1994: .] Error 2
make[2]: *** [/root/linux-6.14/Makefile:251: __sub-make] Error 2
make[2]: Leaving directory '/root/rust-out-of-tree-module'
make[1]: *** [Makefile:251: __sub-make] Error 2
make[1]: Leaving directory '/root/linux-6.14'
make: *** [Makefile:6: default] Error 2

Is there an older kernel I should have used? Would you like a PR if I work out how to update the example repo to work with Linux 6.14?

This seems to fix it (the __LOG_PREFIX errors went away too):

diff --git a/rust_out_of_tree.rs b/rust_out_of_tree.rs
index c8e5cf2..efcb05f 100644
--- a/rust_out_of_tree.rs
+++ b/rust_out_of_tree.rs
@@ -7,7 +7,7 @@ use kernel::prelude::*;
 module! {
     type: RustOutOfTree,
     name: "rust_out_of_tree",
-    authors: ["Rust for Linux Contributors"],
+    author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
     description: "Rust out-of-tree sample",
     license: "GPL",
 }

[  949.109442] rust_out_of_tree: loading out-of-tree module taints kernel.
[  949.114245] rust_out_of_tree: Rust out-of-tree sample (init)
[  957.308801] rust_out_of_tree: My numbers are [72, 108, 200]
[  957.309452] rust_out_of_tree: Rust out-of-tree sample (exit)

Nice

Is there an older kernel I should have used? Would you like a PR if I work out how to update the example repo to work with Linux 6.14?

That repository tracks the mainline kernel, not particular releases. In other words, it is not that the repository needs an update, but rather that it is already updated to use the new way.

Please see the commit log there, e.g. the latest one is the one that changed that to match mainline:

rust_out_of_tree: use the new authors key

Soft-required since 38559da6afb2 ("rust: module: introduce authors key") [1] -- it may be actually required in a kernel cycle or two.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72kHuQxP5J--Y5vtT9d2mJ=0k-xVW+irJzhibAG2BDgSzw@mail.gmail.com/ [1]
Signed-off-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>

https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module/commit/df508ea156314fe281cdaded07bcf89d22c3373a
I hope that helps -- I am glad you made it work!

oh! I see.

maybe you could tag it to match kernel releases?
I considered it in the past, but kernel development should generally be done against mainline, not old releases which are not supported.

so if someone thinks "I wonder how to build a kernel driver in Rust", the only supported solution is to use git head, rather than a release?

That's problematic from a training point of view because it keeps moving.
Not HEAD, but likely the latest Linus' tag.

That's problematic from a training point of view because it keeps moving.

Perhaps, but it is how kernel development works.
it was hard enough making a bootable disk image containing a kernel with Rust support (Ubuntu 24.04's Arm64 kernel apparently does not)
ok, that's fair. They have to meet you where you are.
On top of that, out-of-tree development is not really encouraged, and in particular for Rust, it is best to do it in-tree.
to do that ... they could edit one of the samples?

Or add a new one, yeah. However, it is likely you will want to show abstractions and how they generally work, and for that you want to touch rust/kernel/ code, which you cannot do out-of-tree at the moment.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

it was hard enough making a bootable disk image containing a kernel with Rust support (Ubuntu 24.04's Arm64 kernel apparently does not)

I would recommend using a minimal kernel, not reusing a distribution's configuration.
(if only because it is simpler and takes way less time to build)
the gist currently says to pull the linux-6.14 tarball and do a make LLVM=1 defconfig
I would absolutely recommend using a regular in-tree module as base for training. I don’t think you stand to gain anything by using an out-of-tree module.
happy to hear better ideas. I need to support AArch64 macOS users, and probably Windows x86-64 users.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

the gist currently says to pull the linux-6.14 tarball and do a make LLVM=1 defconfig

It is better to have the actual Git repository around, not a tarball -- it is likely you will want to check old commits and read the Git log and so on while doing development.
(i.e. not you in particular, but teaching them to show how kernel development is usually done)
yeah fair. But download times are worse than the tarball for the limited time I have.
would you git clone https://github.com/torvalds/linux or something else?
You can download a shallow clone, it is fairly quick.
e.g. --shallow-since or --depth or similar options.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

the gist currently says to pull the linux-6.14 tarball and do a make LLVM=1 defconfig

Creating a minimal config that can boot something like Ubuntu or a systemd system in qemu is a bit of a hassle. I can send you what I use for development if you like.
People are actually discussing adding a sat solver to kconfig to be able to select a set of kconfig options to achieve a certain set of features. I am looking forward to that.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

happy to hear better ideas. I need to support AArch64 macOS users, and probably Windows x86-64 users.

I would recommend providing them a Linux VM already prepared with everything they may need (including tools installed, the Git repository fully cloned, etc.). It would save a lot of time if you provide them a way to download it ahead of time.

That way they get the actual development environment people use. While the kernel can be built in macOS with some work, it is not really what people do, much less in Windows.

e.g. Wedson did that in the past with a Virtual Box one.
no, I meant they're running QEMU in macOS
which means they prefer an AArch64 image not an AMD64 one
it's a debian nocloud qcow with all the tools installed (as per the gist)
well right now I show them how to make the qcow. I might give them the option of downloading a ready made qcow, but it it's a trade off between elapsed time and "magic"

I was using multipass, but it hides kernel logs from you and I couldn't work out how to change that. And the kernel I made apparently didn't boot. So I switched to plain qemu where I can see what is going on.

if someone wanted to run a CI job and push out daily / weekly dev environments with all the tools installed and a fresh checkout of the latest kernel (which had already been compiled and used to boot the machine), that would be amazing.
If you are on macOS you probably want two VMs? One for running the build, and one to boot and test your new kernel. As far as I know, there are still some issues with building the kernel in macOS.
I build the kernel in the VM, install it, and reboot it
That is probably not the best approach. Not sure if macOS supports nested virtualization with qemu,but if so, you could spin up a vm inside your vm.

although that does run the risk that if the kernel is bad I might not be able to get back into the machine. Although Debian boots with grub so I think I can just go back to the stock kernel if there's an issue.
performance is bad enough already so not sure I want to attempt nested virtualisation
In that case, maybe share the kernel image back out to macOS and boot a second parallel vm with it. File sharing should be fast enough with virtiofs.
what about kernel modules? How do you get them into the test VM?

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

if someone wanted to run a CI job and push out daily / weekly dev environments with all the tools installed and a fresh checkout of the latest kernel (which had already been compiled and used to boot the machine), that would be amazing.

There are infinite ways to structure a kernel workflow, so it does not really make sense. On top, your workflow is very uncommon. Most kernel developers run some kind of Linux disto and everyone has different preferences on how to set up their environment.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

what about kernel modules? How do you get them into the test VM?

I mount the build output directory via virtiofs into the vm.
yeah, this is not aimed at kernel developers. This is aimed at Rust developers who want to see what it looks like to use Rust in the kernel. I have to give them something that just works.
anyway, I have to run. This has been super helpful, thanks.
There is actually almost an infinite amount of tools to do this process as well. I am using a home grown tool called run-kernel https://github.com/metaspace/run-kernel
Another very popular one is virtme-ng

This is aimed at Rust developers who want to see what it looks like to use Rust in the kernel. I have to give them something that just works.

Something that just works, and that actually shows how kernel development is "typically" done, is to use a distribution like Fedora (possibly in a VM), then dnf install, git clone, configure and build.

If that is too time consuming, you could optimize the steps, use a local mirror, use a pre-built image, and so on and so forth, while keeping it fairly close to what it is usually done.

performance is bad enough already

Hmm... Shouldn't it have near native performance?

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

anyway, I have to run. This has been super helpful, thanks.

You're welcome!
Does Fedora's kernel have Rust support on Arm64?
Ubuntu 24.04's did not.
oh, no, you said git. You'd just pull the kernel tree, build it and then ... sudo make install? That's basically what I'm doing but with Debian.

Hmm... Shouldn't it have near native performance?

QEMU on Windows can use Hyper-V acceleration, and an macOS can use HVF. I don't know that those work once you're inside a QEMU VM.

Fedora builds the kernel with rust, DRM_PANIC_SCREEN_QR_CODE is enabled since 6.12 and I think rust was enabled before. One note though: Fedora builds the C code with gcc diverging from the in-kernel rust instructions

Andreas Hindborg said:

If you are on macOS you probably want two VMs? One for running the build, and one to boot and test your new kernel. As far as I know, there are still some issues with building the kernel in macOS.

You can build kernel on macOS without a VM ;-)
Not on a case-insensitive file system, thought right? :sob:
Right, but you can create a disk file and mount it as a case sensitive filesystem.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

oh, no, you said git. You'd just pull the kernel tree, build it and then ... sudo make install? That's basically what I'm doing but with Debian.

No, I wouldn't install it in the currently running system.

The distribution you are running should not matter much. I mentioned Fedora because that one (and other rolling ones) provides recent Rust releases, and thus you can simply use dnf to install the toolchain instead of using rustup and building bindgen etc.
are there instructions for getting a bootloader, the kernel and its modules into a bootable disk image?
You don’t need all that. You can run qemu with the -kernel flag, skipping the boot loader.
Then pass through you host file system and optionally use overlayfs inside qemu to get ephemeral state.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

Hmm... Shouldn't it have near native performance?

QEMU on Windows can use Hyper-V acceleration, and an macOS can use HVF. I don't know that those work once you're inside a QEMU VM.

Nested virtualization is a thing. You do need to explicitly enable it on the outer VM though and I'm not sure how universally it is supported.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Then pass through you host file system and optionally use overlayfs inside qemu to get ephemeral state.

If there's a blog or some step-by-step README for that, I'd love to read it

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Then pass through you host file system and optionally use overlayfs inside qemu to get ephemeral state.

If there's a blog or some step-by-step README for that, I'd love to read it

I have none of that, but you can take a look here for how to pass virtiofs to qemu.
virtme-ng has an option to pass through the entire host root to a VM and use overlayfs on top of that. Not sure where to look in the source for that.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Then pass through you host file system and optionally use overlayfs inside qemu to get ephemeral state.

If there's a blog or some step-by-step README for that, I'd love to read it

I have none of that, but you can take a look here for how to pass virtiofs to qemu.

You also need to start virtiofsd once for each shared directory, like here
Is it possible to impl Drop for a structure that is tagged #[pin_data]?
the naive attempt tells me no.

please see https://origin.kernel.org/doc/rustdoc/latest/macros/attr.pin_data.html

If your struct implements Drop, then you need to add PinnedDrop as arguments to this macro, and change your Drop implementation to PinnedDrop annotated with #[pinned_drop], since dropping pinned values requires extra care.

I wrote up some instructions on how to make a kernel module in Rust.

The draft is https://rust-for-linux.ferrous-systems-rust-exercises.pages.dev/latest/book/building-linux-kernel-driver.

Once https://github.com/ferrous-systems/rust-exercises/pull/188 is merged, they will appear at https://rust-exercises.ferrous-systems.com/latest/book/building-linux-kernel-driver. The material is Creative Commons and PRs are welcome.

If someone wanted to propose changes to do it as an in-tree module instead of an out-of-tree module, that would be great.
I want to stick to a specific kernel version because I can't keep track of the kernel and make all the necessary changes to this exercise every time the kernel changes.

I ran this training with a client and they were terribly impressed at how 'Rust-y' it all felt, and how there wasn't any unsafe code (at least not that you can see or have to write). Congrats to all involved.
Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) has marked this topic as resolved.

Jonathan Pallant (thejpster/jonathanpallant) said:

I ran this training with a client and they were terribly impressed at how 'Rust-y' it all felt, and how there wasn't any unsafe code (at least not that you can see or have to write). Congrats to all involved.

Thank you! Yeah, all the work on safe abstractions and the policy on "forbidding" direct calls to the C side will hopefully pay off when more and more "leaf" modules get written in safe or mostly safe Rust.
